messages.content:
A researcher intends to use learning analytics to capture, explore and analyse data about learners. However, he is concerned that learning analytics cannot measure transformative learning such as changes in behaviour, beliefs or the understanding of self. Which subjects could provide deeper insight into these issues?


Chemistry and biology

Sociology and psychology

Human geography and politics
Philosophy and literature

To what does the term 'validity' refer?


The accuracy of a measurement and whether tests are measuring what they are supposed to measure

The way that a research instrument is able to yield the same results in repeated trials

An acknowledgement of positionality and bias
The accuracy of a measurement and whether tests are measuring what they are supposed to measure.

Which sentence best describes the term 'multidisciplinary'?


Researchers integrate approaches and methods and transcend traditional disciplinary boundaries

Researchers link different approaches and methods from a variety of disciplines, but have distinct vocabularies and specialisms

Researchers draw from different approaches and methods used in different disciplines and develop a shared research vocabulary

Researchers view approaches and methods from the perspective of another discipline
Researchers integrate approaches and methods and transcend traditional disciplinary boundaries.

If you want to access your data when away from the university, what is the most secure way to do this?


Store data on your university server and use remote services to access the data

Copy data to an unencrypted memory stick

Copy data to your laptop hard drive

Email data to your personal email account
Store data on your university server and use remote services to access the data.

What must you do if you are unable to fully anonymise your research data?


Make them openly available to the public

Only share them using specified access restrictions

Not share them at all

Only share them with interested colleagues
Only share them using specified access restrictions.

When collaborating with others, how can you ensure that the necessary data management, protection, security and sharing procedures are in place?


These issues are covered by your university's data management and sharing policy, so no action is required

You should produce and reach agreement on a plan for data management and sharing

You should ensure that you follow university guidelines and presume others working on the project will do the same
You should produce and reach agreement on a plan for data management and sharing.

If you intend to share data, what should you have in place?


A verbal agreement concerning who is able to access the data

A documented arrangement setting out roles and responsibilities of all those who have access to the data

Nothing, as long as those accessing the data act with integrity
A documented arrangement setting out roles and responsibilities of all those who have access to the data.

Which sentence best describes the term 'interdisciplinary'?


Researchers integrate approaches and methods and transcend traditional disciplinary boundaries

Researchers link different approaches and methods from a variety of disciplines, but have distinct vocabularies and specialisms

Researchers draw from different approaches and methods used in different disciplines and develop a shared research vocabulary

Researchers view approaches and methods from the perspective of another discipline
Researchers draw from different approaches and methods used in different disciplines and develop a shared research vocabulary.

A researcher wants to share their published papers and keep up to date with the work of other researchers in their field. What would be a good tool for them to use?


A presentation tool

An academic networking site

A collaborative search engine

Synchronous video chat
An academic networking site.

When collaborating with others, how can you ensure that the necessary data management, protection, security and sharing procedures are in place?


These issues are covered by your university's data management and sharing policy, so no action is required

You should produce and reach agreement on a plan for data management and sharing

You should ensure that you follow university guidelines and presume others working on the project will do the same
You should produce and reach agreement on a plan for data management and sharing.

If you intend to share data, what should you have in place?


A verbal agreement concerning who is able to access the data

A documented arrangement setting out roles and responsibilities of all those who have access to the data

Nothing, as long as those accessing the data act with integrity
A documented arrangement setting out roles and responsibilities of all those who have access to the data.

What is the name for a document that sets out how original and/or secondary data are to be stored, preserved, shared, transferred, disseminated and disposed of?


A Code of Data Ethics

A Data Management and Sharing Plan (or a Data Management Plan)

A Data Sharing Policy Document (or a Data Sharing Document)

A Data Transfer and Sharing Agreement
A Data Management and Sharing Plan (or a Data Management Plan)

Which of these data collection methods would be most suitable for a researcher who wishes to conduct an ethnographic study with parents and their babies?


Participant observation and unstructured interviews

A parallel-group randomised controlled experiment

Data mining

Online panel research

Closed-ended paper questionnaires
Participant observation and unstructured interviews

A researcher thinks that some interviewees will be nervous and unwilling to open up during the interviews. What is the best way for the researcher to plan for this?


Decide to use questionnaires instead

Find out how to establish rapport and work out how to put participants at their ease prior to the interviews

No planning is required, as the interviewees will relax and open up as the interviews progress

Find and practise some good jokes that can be used to put participants at their ease during the interviews
Find out how to establish rapport and work out how to put participants at their ease prior to the interviews.

Which of the following university offices can help researchers to overcome ethical, legal and policy challenges and dilemmas?


University student funding office or finance department (or equivalent)

University press and publications office or media relations office (or equivalent)

University ethics committee or university governance office (or equivalent)

Human resources or university admissions office (or equivalent)

University counselling service or university welfare office (or equivalent)
University ethics committee or university governance office (or equivalent)

What process or procedure will enable you to examine factors that could cause harm to you and to research participants?


A data analysis action plan

A Data Management Plan

A budget and timetable assessment

A risk assessment

A Code of Ethics
A risk assessment

What is research integrity?


Working within disciplinary standards

Adherence to all ethical principles and professional standards that are required for the responsible conduct of research

A guideline system or framework that includes practices, procedures and rules

Avoidance of error or bias when collecting data

A firm belief in the reliability, truth or ability of researchers
Adherence to all ethical principles and professional standards that are required for the responsible conduct of research.

A researcher wants to analyse transcripts from interviews and focus groups. They would like to identify related content or recurrent topics that convey similar meanings, within and across transcripts. Which would be the most appropriate data analysis method?


Inferential statistics

Historical analysis

Discourse analysis

Thematic analysis

Descriptive statistics
Thematic analysis

How can you protect against losing your data analyses?


Copy your analyses to a memory stick and take it home with you

Adhere to your university's data management and storage regulations and back up all analyses following guidelines

Store your analyses online

Ask your supervisor to keep a copy of your analyses

Email your analyses to yourself
Adhere to your university's data management and storage regulations and back up all analyses following guidelines.

What type of research would enable you to use personal perspective and interpretation to gain insight into people�s motivation and actions, with the aim of describing the phenomenon under study, rather than providing explanations?


Action research

Survey research

Controlled experiments

Phenomenological research

Predictive modelling
Phenomenological research

What is the name of the European Union's data privacy and security law that came into force in 2018?


Human Rights Act

General Data Protection Regulation

Freedom of Information Act

Data Protection Act

Telecommunications (Data Protection and Privacy) Regulations
General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)

Rigorous retests of social science research are failing to replicate or reproduce the original study. How can you make it easier for others to replicate your research?


Ensure clear, accurate and freely available documentation of all steps of my research process

Replication is not necessary if my methods and analyses are sound

Only provide details of my analysis when other researchers want to replicate my work

Write a paper and submit it to a high-ranking journal

Write a paper and place it in my university repository
Ensure clear, accurate and freely available documentation of all steps of my research process.

Which of the following should you be able to do in your final research submission?


Make implicit, rather than explicit, references to methodology

Describe and explain your choice of methodology

Discuss your research without reference to methodology
Describe and explain your choice of methodology.

Which of the following can be an academic and intellectual challenge when planning interdisciplinary and interinstitutional research?


Deciding which software to use

Planning the research schedule

Addressing different research vocabularies and worldviews
Addressing different research vocabularies and worldviews.

Which of the following are secondary sources?


Performances and paintings

Interview transcripts

Articles analysing the artworks you are researching
Articles analysing the artworks you are researching.

What does research design involve?


Aligning research methods and processes with your research question

Retrospectively deciding what research methodology you have used after completing your project

Producing a well-formatted submission at the end of your project
Aligning research methods and processes with your research question.

What should you do first if you are concerned you will have difficulty agreeing times to conduct interviews for your research?


Change your research question to avoid this issue

Change your research plan so you are conducting fewer interviews

Contact the participants well in advance and offer telephone or online alternatives
Contact the participants well in advance and offer telephone or online alternatives.

A continuous glucose monitoring system captures which type of data?


Observational data

Secondary data

Simulation data

Derived data
Observational data

Which of the following sampling methods is least prone to bias?


Simple random sampling

Quasi-random (or systematic) sampling

Convenience sampling

Cluster sampling
Simple random sampling

What is the confidence level?


The probability that the true value will lie within the confidence interval

An expression of the variability of the population

The prevalence rate in the population

The acceptable level of a false positive
The probability that the true value will lie within the confidence interval.

What is validation?


Obtaining similar results when the same analysis is repeated

Confirming a result is statistically significant

Confirming the accuracy of the measurement instrument

Confirming an initial result using an alternative, independent method
Confirming an initial result using an alternative, independent method.

What type of research enables a researcher to collect data in the subject's natural environment?


Laboratory experiments

Focus group interviews

Data mining

Field research
Field research

What is the 3-2-1 data storage rule?


Three copies stored across two different media, with one in a separate physical location

Three types of media in two physical locations, accessible by one researcher

One type of media in at least two locations, accessible by three researchers

Three researchers storing two copies of all data in one physical location
Three copies stored across two different media, with one in a separate physical location.

What is cluster analysis used for?


Hypothesis testing to estimate differences between groups

Uncovering groups within a data set

Testing cause-and-effect relationship between variables

Identifying differences between the mean values of groups
Uncovering groups within a data set.

Which of the following is an example of bias?


Choosing software because you are familiar with the interface

Randomly allocating clinical trial participants to the treatment or placebo group

Using control samples to test for confounding variables

Validating your results with more data sources
Choosing software because you are familiar with the interface.

In the 3Rs governing use of animals in research, what is refinement?


Development and use of alternative, non-animal models

Using methods that minimise the number of animals required

Using methods that minimise pain, suffering and harm to animals

Reducing time researchers are in contact with animals
Using methods that minimise pain, suffering and harm to animals.

What are the core elements of research integrity?


Competition, rivalry, contest and championship

Sympathy, empathy, harmony, conformity and consensus

Repute, eminence, acclaim and distinction

Honesty, rigour, transparency, care and respect, and accountability
Honesty, rigour, transparency, care and respect, and accountability.

What are descriptive statistics used for?


Summarising features of a data set

Identifying statistically significant differences between groups

Uncovering cause-and-effect relationships

Drawing conclusions about a population based on a sample
Descriptive statistics are used for summarising features of a data set.

Which would be an appropriate format to illustrate data trends?


A volumetric image

A topographic scan

A network visualisation

A bar graph
A bar graph.

What should you do when you are critically evaluating research methods?


Discuss options with your supervisors

Only inform your supervisors when you have made choices

Only focus on methods you are already familiar with
Discuss options with your supervisors.

What does critical thinking involve?


Arguing against the opinions of others

Analysing and discussing different opinions

Arguing only against the opinions that do not align with your own
Analysing and discussing different opinions.

What should the submission at the end of your research do?


Form a coherent whole

Address the individual stages of the research process separately

Provide as many questions as answers
Form a coherent whole.

Are ethical decisions always governed by the law?


Yes, they are always aligned with and based on the law

No, ethical decisions are based only on moral, not legal, standards

No, although some are, not all ethical considerations are governed by the law
No, although some are, not all ethical considerations are governed by the law.

What is the term used to describe the distortion, manipulation or false representation of sources, methods or findings?


Plagiarism

Fabrication

Falsification
Falsification

An advantage of presenting qualitative data is:


It provides depth, detail and understanding

It can be influenced by the researcher's personal bias

It can be interpreted in different ways
It provides depth, detail, and understanding.

Which data reduction technique would a researcher be applying when grouping together similar responses into themes?


Open coding

Closed coding

Axial coding
Axial coding

The amount of money in a bank account is an example of which type of quantitative data?


Interval data

Ratio data

Ordinal data
Ratio data

The ranking of people's preference for different flavours of crisp flavours is an example of which type of quantitative data?


Ordinal data

Nominal data

Interval data
Ordinal data

Which of the following is a property of interval data?


The gap between two values is the same as two other values on an ordered scale

The gap between two values is the same as two other values on an ordered scale and there is an absolute zero value

The gap between 20 and 30 is twice the value of the gap between 10 and 20
The gap between two values is the same as two other values on an ordered scale.

The mean of a sample is:


The total of all the results added together, and then divided by the amount of results collected

The score that occurs most often

The middle number of a set of ordered numbers
The total of all the results added together, and then divided by the amount of results collected.

Which of the following is another name for the Gaussian curve?


An average distribution curve

A chi-squared distribution

An inverse curve
An inverse curve

The standard deviation of +1 to -1 accounts for:


68.2% of the population

95.3% of the population

99.6% of the population
68.2% of the population

Which of the following best describes the p-value of a statistical test?


The probability of the findings occurring by chance

The plausibility of the results in relation to the literature

The prevalence of the highest score from the results
The probability of the findings occurring by chance.

Non-parametric tests are used:


When two different scores, perhaps taken at different times, are compared with two different scores taken from another group

To compare the means of two different groups who have been measured using the same test

Usually when the sample size is less than 30
Usually when the sample size is less than 30

What is the core purpose of you undertaking a research project and delivering a dissertation?


To demonstrate that you can work with others and process the collected data

To show that you are aware of the current debates within the literature

To provide an answer to a question you have set
To provide an answer to a question you have set.

Comparing the performance of one country to another to make an argument is what type of argument structure?


Argument by example

Argument by analogy

Argument by authority
Argument by analogy

Which of the following is an argument based on making a series of links between the evidence and conclusions?


A logical argument

A basic argument

Argument about cause and effect
A logical argument

Which of the following is an argument by authority?


According to research, the last universal common ancestor is about 4 billion years old

Research by an eminent professor of biology reports that the last universal common ancestor is about 4 billion years old

Studies have demonstrated that the last universal common ancestor is about 4 billion years old
Research by an eminent professor of biology reports that the last universal common ancestor is about 4 billion years old.

What should be included in the methodology section of a dissertation?


How data were collected

An identification of the potential gaps in knowledge using a literature review

The core themes and trends within the topic being researched
How data were collected

Which chapter would be the most appropriate to include a discussion of ethics?


The literature review

The methodology chapter

The results chapter
The methodology chapter
